title: A small repair that saved an object from the bin
summary: Narrate the story of repairing something instead of throwing it away, and argue whether repair is a skill worth learning in the modern world.
topic: Repairing an object instead of replacing it
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) narrating the story of a time you — or someone you know — repaired an object instead of discarding it. Describe the object, the repair, and how the outcome felt. Then argue briefly whether learning to repair things should be more encouraged. Use the past simple and present perfect, and at least one modal to make your argument.
hintStarters: The object was not worth much to anyone else, but I had…
What surprised me most about the repair was that…
If more people knew how to fix things, we would probably…

hintVocab.meaningVi: lắp ráp lại vật gì đó
thợ đóng giày, thợ sửa giày
giữ nguyên, không bị tách ra

hintVocab.phrase: to buy cheaply and replace often
hintVocab.meaningVi: mua rẻ và thay thế thường xuyên
truyền lại cho thế hệ sau

sampleText:
My grandmother had owned the same pair of leather sandals for as long as I could remember. She had bought them in a market in the city where she was born and had worn them through three decades of daily life — to the market, to family gatherings, to the small garden behind the house. The leather had darkened to the colour of old wood, and one of the straps had started to split at the buckle. To me they looked finished — worn past the point where repair seemed worth the effort. To her, they were simply shoes that needed attention. She took them to a cobbler two streets away, a quiet man who worked at a bench in an open doorway with a row of awls and needles arranged by size on a strip of card. He looked at the strap for about four seconds, then stitched it back with a doubled thread and tapped a small rivet into the split. It took him eleven minutes. My grandmother paid him less than the cost of a coffee and walked home in her fixed sandals.

The thing that stayed with me was how the repair changed the shoes, or rather how it changed the way I saw them. They were not good as new — the leather still showed the creases and scuffs of decades — but they had a quality that a new pair would have taken years to develop. The cobbler had also, without being asked, oiled the entire sole and re-tightened the other buckle. My grandmother wore those sandals for another four years before the cobbler retired and she could not find anyone else she trusted with them.

That cobbler has since closed his doorway bench, and the repair shops on the streets nearby have gradually disappeared. What has replaced them is a row of phone cases and cheap trainers sold from plastic shelves. This is not a coincidence. As the cost of making new things has fallen, the economic case for repairing old ones has weakened, and a generation has grown up without knowing how to maintain the objects they own. The result is a particular kind of helplessness — not dramatic, but quiet and cumulative. Some schools have begun to reintroduce practical skills — woodwork, basic electronics, textiles — and this seems to me exactly the right direction. If more people could mend a strap, re-solder a wire, or patch a torn seam, fewer objects would end up discarded after a single fault. The skill itself is not the main point. The main point is the habit of asking whether something is worth saving before deciding it is not.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to set the scene before the main story
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'My grandmother had owned the same pair of leather sandals for as long as I could remember' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành (had + past participle) để diễn tả điều gì đó đã đúng trước khi câu chuyện chính bắt đầu. Nó thiết lập bối cảnh mà không làm gián đoạn dòng sự kiện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'My grandmother had owned the same pair of leather sandals for as long as I could remember' uses the past perfect (had + past participle) to describe something true before the main story begins. It sets the scene without interrupting the flow of events.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'could' in a conditional to argue a practical point
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If more people could mend a strap, re-solder a wire, or patch a torn seam, fewer objects would end up discarded' dùng điều kiện loại 2 với 'could' để lập luận về một điều có thể xảy ra. 'Could' ở đây không có nghĩa là khả năng mà là kỹ năng — nếu mọi người có kỹ năng đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If more people could mend a strap, re-solder a wire, or patch a torn seam, fewer objects would end up discarded' uses the second conditional with 'could' for ability — not possibility. It argues that if people had the skill, a different outcome would follow.

sampleTranslationVi:
Bà tôi đã sở hữu đôi dép da giống nhau từ khi tôi còn có thể nhớ. Da đã chuyển sang màu gỗ cũ, và một trong những quai dép đã bắt đầu bị nứt ở khóa. Với tôi, chúng trông như đã xong — mòn đến mức việc sửa dường như không còn đáng công. Với bà, chúng chỉ là đôi giày cần được chăm chút. Bà mang chúng đến thợ đóng giày hai con phố bên, một người đàn ông trầm lặng làm việc ở ngưỡng cửa mở với hàng dùi và kim được sắp xếp theo kích cỡ trên một dải giấy các tông. Ông nhìn chiếc quai khoảng bốn giây, rồi khâu lại bằng sợi chỉ đôi và gõ một đinh tán nhỏ vào chỗ nứt. Ông mất mười một phút. Bà tôi trả ông ít hơn giá một ly cà phê và đi bộ về nhà với đôi dép đã được sửa.

Điều khiến tôi ấn tượng là cách việc sửa chữa đã thay đổi đôi giày. Chúng không tốt như mới — da vẫn còn những nếp nhăn và vết trầy xước của hàng chục năm — nhưng chúng có một chất lượng mà một đôi mới sẽ mất nhiều năm để có được. Người thợ đóng giày cũng, mà không cần được yêu cầu, đã bôi dầu toàn bộ đế và siết chặt lại chiếc khóa kia. Bà tôi đã đi đôi dép đó thêm bốn năm trước khi người thợ nghỉ hưu và bà không tìm được ai khác bà tin tưởng với chúng.

Người thợ đóng giày đó kể từ đó đã đóng cửa băng ghế ở ngưỡng cửa, và các tiệm sửa chữa trên các con phố lân cận dần dần biến mất. Thứ thay thế chúng là một hàng ốp điện thoại và giày thể thao rẻ tiền bày trên kệ nhựa. Đây không phải là sự trùng hợp. Khi chi phí sản xuất đồ mới giảm xuống, lý do kinh tế để sửa đồ cũ đã yếu đi, và một thế hệ đã lớn lên mà không biết cách bảo dưỡng những đồ vật họ sở hữu. Một số trường học đã bắt đầu tái giới thiệu các kỹ năng thực tiễn — mộc, điện tử cơ bản, dệt may — và điều này với tôi có vẻ là hướng đi đúng đắn. Nếu nhiều người có thể vá một quai, hàn lại một dây điện, hoặc vá một đường may rách, ít đồ vật hơn sẽ cuối cùng bị vứt bỏ sau một lỗi duy nhất. Bản thân kỹ năng không phải là điểm chính. Điểm chính là thói quen hỏi liệu điều gì đó có đáng được cứu trước khi quyết định rằng không.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect to describe a state before the main story?
options: She had owned the same sandals for as long as I could remember.
She owned the same sandals for as long as I could remember.
She has owned the same sandals for as long as I could remember.
She had been owning the same sandals for as long as I could remember.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect ('had owned') sets a background state that existed before the narrative's main time. Simple past ('owned') can work but lacks the sense of duration into the past. Present perfect ('has owned') shifts to the present. 'Had been owning' is awkward with stative verbs like 'own'.
mcQuestions.hint: Stative verbs like 'own' usually avoid the progressive form, even in the past perfect.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'seem' correctly with an adjective complement?
options: To me, the shoes looked finished — worn past the point where repair seemed worth the effort.
To me, the shoes looked finished — worn past the point where repair seemed worthy the effort.
To me, the shoes looked finished — worn past the point where repair seemed to worth the effort.
To me, the shoes looked finished — worn past the point where repair seemed worthing the effort.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Seemed worth the effort' uses 'worth' as an adjective/preposition correctly. 'Seemed worthy the effort' is missing 'of'. 'Seemed to worth' is not a standard construction. 'Worthing' is not a valid word.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Worth the effort' is a fixed phrase — 'worth' here is not a verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past simple to narrate a sequence of actions?
options: He stitched it back with a doubled thread and tapped a small rivet into the split.
He stitched it back with a doubled thread and has tapped a small rivet into the split.
He had stitched it back with a doubled thread and tapped a small rivet into the split.
He stitched it back with a doubled thread and was tapping a small rivet into the split.
mcQuestions.explanation: Sequential past actions use past simple throughout. Mixing past perfect ('had stitched') suggests the first action happened before another reference point. Mixing present perfect or past progressive breaks the even narrative rhythm.
mcQuestions.hint: For two actions in the same quick sequence, keep both verbs in the past simple.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'since' correctly with the present perfect?
options: The cobbler has since closed his doorway bench.
The cobbler has closed his doorway bench since.
The cobbler since has closed his doorway bench.
The cobbler has since been closing his doorway bench.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has since closed' = present perfect with 'since' meaning 'from that time until now'. Placing 'since' at the end is grammatically odd and sounds incomplete. Placing it before the auxiliary is incorrect English word order. The progressive 'has since been closing' implies an ongoing or repeated process, which is wrong here.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Since' as an adverb of 'from that time' sits between the auxiliary 'has' and the past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'as … has fallen' with the present perfect?
options: As the cost of making new things has fallen, the case for repair has weakened.
As the cost of making new things fallen, the case for repair has weakened.
As the cost of making new things have fallen, the case for repair has weakened.
As the cost of making new things has fallen, the case for repair weakened.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has fallen' is present perfect for 'the cost' (singular). Omitting 'has' leaves no auxiliary verb. 'Have fallen' is wrong because 'cost' is singular. The main clause should also use present perfect to match the timeframe — 'weakened' (simple past) is inconsistent.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Cost' is a singular noun — it takes 'has', not 'have'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a second conditional correctly to argue a practical point?
options: If more people could mend a strap, fewer objects would end up discarded.
If more people can mend a strap, fewer objects would end up discarded.
If more people could mend a strap, fewer objects will end up discarded.
If more people could mend a strap, fewer objects would end up to be discarded.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: 'If + past simple/could, … would + bare infinitive'. Using 'can' in the 'if' clause is first conditional (more likely/real). Using 'will' in the main clause is also first conditional. 'Would end up to be discarded' is non-standard.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional mixes could/past in the 'if' clause with 'would + base verb' in the result.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'worth' as a preposition?
options: The skill of repair is worth learning.
The skill of repair is worth to learn.
The skill of repair is worth to be learning.
The skill of repair is worthy learning.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Worth + gerund (-ing)' is the correct construction. 'Worth to learn' uses an infinitive, which is ungrammatical after 'worth'. 'Worth to be learning' is doubly wrong. 'Worthy' is a different adjective and must be followed by 'of', not a bare gerund.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Worth' is followed by a gerund (-ing form), not an infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes objects being thrown away after one problem?
options: end up discarded after a single fault
end up being discarded after a unique fault
end up to be discarded after a single fault
end up discarded after only one fault thing
mcQuestions.explanation: 'End up + past participle' is a natural passive construction. 'End up being discarded' is also grammatical but slightly heavier. 'End up to be' is incorrect. 'A single fault' is the natural collocation; 'a unique fault' has a different meaning and 'fault thing' is non-native.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'end up', use either a past participle directly or an -ing form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'without being asked' as a participial phrase?
options: The cobbler, without being asked, oiled the entire sole.
The cobbler, without be asking, oiled the entire sole.
The cobbler, without to be asked, oiled the entire sole.
The cobbler, without asked, oiled the entire sole.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Without + gerund' (without being asked) is the correct pattern. 'Without be asking' uses the base form incorrectly. 'Without to be' mixes the infinitive pattern with a preposition. 'Without asked' uses a past participle without 'being'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'without', use a gerund phrase: 'without being + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'has grown up' to describe a generation?
options: A generation has grown up without knowing how to maintain their objects.
A generation have grown up without knowing how to maintain their objects.
A generation has grown up without to know how to maintain their objects.
A generation has grew up without knowing how to maintain their objects.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A generation' is treated as a singular noun in formal writing, so 'has' is correct. 'Have grown up' treats it as plural, which is less formal. 'Without to know' is ungrammatical — 'without' needs a gerund. 'Has grew' uses simple past after an auxiliary — the past participle 'grown' is required.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'has', use the past participle — the past participle of 'grow up' is 'grown up'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'asking' correctly as part of a gerund phrase to describe the habit?
options: The main point is the habit of asking whether something is worth saving.
The main point is the habit of to ask whether something is worth saving.
The main point is the habit asking whether something is worth saving.
The main point is the habit for asking whether something is worth saving.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The habit of + gerund' is the correct pattern. 'The habit of to ask' mixes a preposition with an infinitive. 'The habit asking' needs the preposition 'of'. 'The habit for asking' uses the wrong preposition — 'for' is not idiomatic with 'habit' in this sense.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed collocation is 'the habit of + -ing form'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there an object you own that you have repaired or would repair rather than replace? What is it about that object — its age, its associations, or something else — that makes it worth the effort?
